title: When Using AI Leads to “Brain Fry”
subtitle: A new study finds that certain patterns of AI use are driving cognitive fatigue, while others can help reduce burnout.

content_blocks.title: The Performance and Retention Costs of AI Brain Fry

items.label: Increase in decision fatigue among workers experiencing brain fry
Higher frequency of major errors (safety, outcomes, or decisions)
Active intent to leave among workers with AI brain fry vs 25% without
Reduction in burnout when AI is used to replace routine tasks

content_blocks.title: Distinguishing Emotional Burnout from Cognitive Brain Fry

items.a: State of chronic workplace stress involving emotional exhaustion.
items.b: Acute mental fatigue caused by marshalling attention beyond cognitive capacity.

items.a: Lowered when AI is used to replace repetitive or unenjoyable tasks.
items.b: Predictably increased by high degrees of AI oversight and monitoring.

items.a: Typically characterized by negative feelings about one's job.
items.b: Characterized by 'mental fog,' 'buzzing' sensations, and headaches.

content_blocks.title: Organizational and Behavioral Drivers of Mental Fatigue
points: Productivity peaks when using two to three AI tools simultaneously, but scores dip significantly when a fourth tool is added.
High AI oversight—the direct monitoring of agents—is the most taxing engagement, predicting 19% greater information overload.
Managerial support is a critical buffer: workers whose managers answer AI questions report 15% lower mental fatigue scores.
The 'AI orphan tax' occurs when employees are expected to figure out tools on their own, resulting in measurably higher fatigue.
Corporate messaging regarding AI-boosted efficiency often signals unintended 'work intensification' to employees.
